title: Thermalization with Gaussian Quantum Cellular Automata
abstract: We study the long-time dynamics of many-body bosonic lattice systems under translation-invariant Gaussian quantum cellular automata. We formulate two sets of conditions on GQCAs which separately guarantee thermalization of any state on the local Weyl algebra to the infinite temperature state, whenever the state is locally normal and has uniformly bounded particle density. Our main intermediate result is a quantum many-body generalization of the classic Riemann-Lebesgue lemma which is a bound on expectation values of local Weyl operators involving their support and the state's particle density.
summary: The paper rigorously proves that many-body bosonic lattice systems evolving under Gaussian Quantum Cellular Automata (GQCAs) thermalize to infinite temperature if the dynamics exhibit sufficient hyperbolic properties. The proof relies on advanced techniques from functional analysis, generalizing the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma to show that local observables lose memory of their initial state over time.
main_problem: The central problem is rigorously establishing conditions on quantum dynamics (Gaussian Quantum Cellular Automata) that guarantee thermalization—the approach to infinite temperature equilibrium—for many-body bosonic lattice systems.
main_result: Thermalization is proven under two sufficient conditions: global hyperbolicity or local hyperbolicity combined with regularity, leading to the decay of expectation values of local Weyl operators towards the infinite-temperature state.
method: The analysis uses techniques from functional analysis and operator theory, generalizing the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma to bound expectation values $\omega(W(\xi_k))$ by exploiting exponential growth in phase space combined with finite density moment conditions.
model_or_system: The system consists of bosonic lattice systems on $\mathbb{Z}^d$ whose time evolution is governed by translation-invariant Gaussian Quantum Cellular Automata (GQCAs). The dynamics are analyzed via the action on local Weyl operators $W(\xi)$.
key_observables: Local Weyl operators $\omega(W(\xi))$, expectation values of local observables, and the particle density moment $\omega_{ho_\Gamma}(N_\Gamma)$.
important_parameters:
The lattice dimension $d$, the hyperbolicity constants ($\gamma$), and the uniform bound on the particle density $
u$.
main_assumptions: The initial state must be locally normal, and the dynamics must satisfy specific hyperbolic conditions (global or local) related to the symplectic map $\Phi$ governing the evolution.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes; the structure relies on theorems and lemmas establishing decay rates based on spectral properties of the underlying maps.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the necessary mathematical tools (Weyl algebra, local normality) to deriving key estimates (Riemann-Lebesgue generalization via commutator norms), culminating in proving thermalization under specific hyperbolic conditions for the GQCA dynamics.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous, mathematically deep criteria for when quantum systems governed by simple, local rules will reach equilibrium, which is a cornerstone problem in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics and quantum information theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 LH, UH, R, and E stand for the local and uniform hyperbolicity, regularity and everydayness, respectively. The shaded region LH ∩R contains the GQCAs for which we prove thermalization.
